text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to take down adhesions between the omentum and the liver, including some perinephric fat. The dissection of the gallbladder begins on the lateral side, with visible use of ICG for enhanced visualization. A large stone is lodged in the neck of the gallbladder, complicating its manipulation for exposure.
The surgeon uses scissors and a hook to manipulate the gallbladder during the cholecystectomy. The common bile duct is visible under ICG mode, and the surgeon targets the cystic duct for dissection.

The surgeon encounters difficulty dissecting the medial side of the gallbladder and decides to return to the lateral side for better exposure. The gallbladder is flipped to create a window behind it, freeing the tissue up to the top of the gallbladder. The surgeon attempts to isolate the cystic duct and artery junction, noting the artery runs close to the duct. Due to manipulation challenges, the surgeon adopts a top-down approach to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, improving exposure of the cystic artery above the duct.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adventitial tissue surrounding the cystic artery and cystic duct complex, aiming to expose the junctional point with the common bile duct. The cystic duct and artery are visible, and the surgeon prepares to place clips on the artery.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the cystic artery on the specimen side, using focused bipolar cautery to achieve hemostasis. With good exposure from previous mobilization, a window is created under the artery, allowing for the application of clips.

The surgeon thins out the tissue surrounding the wide cystic duct, preparing it for clip placement. As the dissection progresses closer to the junction with the common bile duct, the duct narrows to an appropriate caliber for clipping. The critical view is clearly visible, showing the cystic duct alongside the common bile duct and common hepatic ducts. Clips are applied to the cystic duct, which is then transected.
